course_overview: This unit teaches candidates how to answer comprehension questions from passages about electricity, energy, power systems, rural electrification, planning, health, education, and development.
key_points: Read the title, source, and first paragraph to identify the topic quickly. | Underline technical words such as generation, transmission, distribution, reliability, affordability, investment, demand, and rural electrification. | Read the question carefully and identify its focus word: what, why, how, who, when, or which factor. | Scan the passage for the matching idea, then read two or three lines before and after it. | Answer in one clear sentence unless the question asks for more detail. | Do not copy a long paragraph; select the relevant idea and rewrite it in your own words. | For “why” questions, include a reason. For “how” questions, explain the process or effect. | If the passage gives examples, use them briefly to support your answer.
example_1_text: Question focus: “Why is reliable electricity important in health services?”
example_2_text: It is important for health services.
example_3_text: Reliable electricity is important in health services because it allows clinics and hospitals to store vaccines, use medical equipment, and serve more patients safely.
example_4_text: Question focus: “How does electricity support education?”
example_5_text: Electricity supports education by providing lighting for study, powering computers and media devices, and improving students’ learning experience.

course_title: 2. Core Grammar Patterns for Rewrite Questions
course_overview: This unit covers the main sentence patterns commonly tested in rewrite questions. Candidates should keep the original meaning while changing the structure correctly.
key_points: Keep the meaning unchanged when rewriting. | Check tense, subject-verb agreement, articles, and punctuation after rewriting. | Use linking expressions that match the relationship: addition, contrast, reason, result, purpose, comparison, or condition. | In rewrite questions, grammar accuracy is more important than using difficult words.
example_1_text: The ministry installed new transformers. → New transformers were installed by the ministry.
example_2_text: He said, “Work carefully.” → He told me to work carefully.
example_3_text: Ko Ko studies English. Su Su studies English. → Both Ko Ko and Su Su study English.
example_4_text: The cable is very heavy. We cannot lift it. → The cable is so heavy that we cannot lift it.
example_5_text: Although it was raining, they continued the survey. → In spite of the rain, they continued the survey.

course_title: 3. Grammar Pattern Bank with More Examples
course_overview: Use this pattern bank as a quick revision guide before answering the rewrite section.
key_points: Active → Passive: object becomes subject; use correct form of be + past participle. | Direct → Indirect: change tense, pronouns, and time words when necessary. | Can / Could / May: use modals correctly after the subject; the base verb follows. | Too...to / Enough to: use them to express degree and ability. | Either...or / Neither...nor: the verb agrees with the nearer subject in formal usage. | As...as / Not as...as: use for equal or unequal comparison. | Because / Because of: use because + clause; because of + noun phrase. | If clauses: If + present, will + base verb for real future conditions. | Used to / Be used to / Be used for: do not confuse past habit, familiarity, and purpose. | To + infinitive: often shows purpose, plan, or result.
example_1_text: The staff maintain the feeder lines regularly. → The feeder lines are maintained regularly by the staff.
example_2_text: The manager said, “The team has finished the report.” → The manager said that the team had finished the report.
example_3_text: You can submit the report tomorrow.
example_4_text: The load is too high to be supplied by one small transformer.
example_5_text: The room is large enough to hold the training session.

course_overview: Articles are often tested in correction and rewrite questions. Correct article use makes sentences sound natural and precise.
key_points: Use a before a singular countable noun beginning with a consonant sound: a meter, a feeder, a village. | Use an before a singular countable noun beginning with a vowel sound: an engineer, an operator, an hour. | Use the for something specific, previously mentioned, or unique in context: the ministry, the national grid, the project office. | Do not use an article before plural or uncountable nouns when speaking generally: Electricity is essential. Engineers need experience. | Use the when both writer and reader know which thing is meant: the report, the damaged line, the township hospital.
example_2_text: The operator checked the control panel.
example_3_text: A village clinic needs reliable electricity.
example_5_text: He bought a equipment. ✗ → He bought equipment. / He bought a piece of equipment. ✓

course_overview: Prepositions are small words, but they are tested frequently. Learn them in useful patterns instead of studying them one by one.
key_points: Use in for years, months, towns, countries, and enclosed places: in 2025, in June, in Mandalay, in the office. | Use on for days and dates: on Monday, on 15 July. | Use at for exact time and precise points: at 8:30 a.m., at the substation, at the meeting. | Use to for movement, direction, and connection: go to Nay Pyi Taw, connect to the grid. | Use for for purpose, duration, and benefit: used for irrigation, for two years, important for farmers. | Use from...to for range: from Yangon to Bago. | Use between for two items; among for more than two. | Common exam phrases: interested in, responsible for, satisfied with, depend on, increase in, reduction in, access to, related to.
example_1_text: The officer arrived at the office at 9 a.m. on Monday.
example_2_text: The village was connected to the grid in 2024.
example_3_text: This pump is used for agricultural work.
example_4_text: There is a close relationship between energy use and development.
example_5_text: Consumers are satisfied with the new service.

course_overview: Fill-in-the-blank questions test vocabulary, grammar, collocation, and logical meaning. Many items in this exam come from formal or technical English.
key_points: Read the whole sentence before choosing a word. | Notice grammar clues such as article, noun, verb, adjective, adverb, singular/plural, and preposition. | Check whether the blank needs a content word (investment, service, demand) or a function word (in, of, because). | Use the surrounding words to test natural combinations: capital-intensive industry, reliable supply, financial resources, distribution system. | After filling the blank, read the sentence again to check both grammar and meaning.
example_1_text: Power development is difficult because electric power industries are capital-intensive.
example_2_text: The utility must provide reliable service to consumers.
example_3_text: Distribution planners determine the location of substations.
example_4_text: Importing oil is a drain on foreign exchange reserves.
example_5_text: If the blank comes after “because”, a clause usually follows. If it comes after “because of”, a noun phrase follows.

course_overview: Essay questions require clear organization, relevant ideas, and correct grammar. Candidates do not need very difficult vocabulary; they need a clear structure and strong examples.
key_points: Write an introduction that states the main idea directly. | Use two or three body paragraphs. Each paragraph should develop one main point. | Use linking words such as firstly, moreover, in addition, therefore, finally. | Support ideas with electricity-related examples: health services, education, businesses, irrigation, communication, industry. | End with a conclusion that restates the main point in different words. | Keep within the word limit and avoid repeating the same sentence pattern many times.
example_1_text: Increased access to electricity plays an important role in national development because it improves both economic activities and people’s quality of life.
example_2_text: Firstly, electricity supports businesses by extending working hours, reducing losses, and improving productivity.
example_3_text: Moreover, it benefits social sectors such as health and education by powering clinics, classrooms, and communication systems.
example_4_text: For these reasons, reliable electricity should be expanded throughout the country.
example_5_text: Firstly / Secondly / Moreover / In addition / Therefore / As a result / In conclusion

course_overview: Letter questions usually require a formal format, polite tone, and clear purpose. Candidates should memorize the page layout and a few useful sentences.
key_points: Write the sender’s address at the top left or top right, depending on the format your exam practice uses. | Write the date clearly below the address. | Write the receiver’s designation and office if required. | Add a subject line when writing an official request or report. | Use a formal greeting: Dear Sir, Dear Sir/Madam, or Dear Director. | Paragraph 1: state why you are writing. | Paragraph 2: explain the problem or request with relevant details. | Paragraph 3: ask politely for action and close respectfully. | Use a formal closing: Yours faithfully, Yours sincerely, Respectfully yours.
example_1_text: I am writing to request urgent repair work on the damaged distribution line in our township.
example_2_text: Due to the fault, several villages have been facing power interruptions during the evening peak period.
example_3_text: I would be grateful if the department could arrange maintenance work as soon as possible.
example_4_text: Thank you for your attention to this matter.
example_5_text:
Yours faithfully,
Aung Min
Assistant Engineer

course_overview: This page gives learners a page-like letter format they can memorize for exam use.
key_points: Write address first. | Add date clearly. | Include receiver information if required. | Use a subject line for official letters. | Start with a formal salutation. | State purpose in the first paragraph. | Give details in the second paragraph. | Make a polite request in the final paragraph. | Close with a formal ending and signature.
example_1_text: Sender address → Date → Receiver → Subject → Salutation
example_2_text: Purpose paragraph → Detail paragraph → Request paragraph
example_4_text: Subject: Request for replacement of damaged distribution poles
example_5_text: I am writing to request the replacement of several damaged distribution poles along the Myaing–Kamma feeder.

course_overview: Fast revision page for common sentence patterns.
key_points: Although + clause | In spite of + noun phrase | So...that | Too...to | Enough to | Not only...but also | Neither...nor | Either...or | As soon as | In order to
example_1_text: Although the weather was bad, the team continued the inspection.
example_2_text: In spite of the bad weather, the team continued the inspection.
example_3_text: The conductor was too short to reach the next pole.
example_4_text: The explanation is clear enough to understand.
example_5_text: Electricity improves not only industry but also health services.
